sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten notelet from Mandyczewski to Schenker, dated November 9, 1912
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Mandyczewski: the visit to the Wittgensteins' house is November 12.

tei:opener.tei:salute: Verehrter Hr Dr.!Receipt of this notelet is not recorded in Schenker’s diary.

tei:div.tei:p: Bitte am Dienstag, d.i. 12. Nov. Pünkt. 4 Uhr beim Hause IV Alleegasse 16 zu sein. Schenker records the visit to the Wittgenstein household on November 12: “With Dr. Mandyczewski at Wittgenstein's, an examination of the autograph of Op. 109. Quite unexpectedly surprising discoveries, which confirm everything I have taught my pupils in spite of the corruption of the text. Nottebohm's and Mandyczewski's notes prove to be too superficial, and do not come close to the heart of the matter. / Dr. Mandyczewski is concerned about leaving me on my own for a while with Mr. Paul Wittgenstein! He was not keen to see the young gentleman becoming better acquainted with me. He suggested that I depart with him and return the following Tuesday, and he was himself on his way out when Mr. Wittgenstein said that he, too, was prepared to join him. And so I was able to study the autograph for another hour, on my own and undisturbed.” Da wohnen Wittgensteins. Man erwartet uns dort um diese Zeit. Ich komme 4 Uhr, und wir gehn gleich hinein u. hinauf. A subsequent visit is recorded in Schenker’s diary for November 19, 1912: “With Mandyczewski at Wittgenstein's. The autograph manuscript copied for over three hours, and interesting new presentable versions played to the two gentlemen.”
Verzeihn Sie diesen Zettel der Eile

We are bidden to be at house No. 4, Alleegasse 16, on Tuesday, November 12, punctually at 4 p.m. That’s where the Wittgensteins live. We are expected there at that time. I will be there at 4 p.m., and we will go in and upstairs.
Please forgive this hasty scrap of paper
